description: The Jerusalem Council convened to decide whether Gentile converts required circumcision and observance of Mosaic law.
significance: Established the principle that Gentile Christians were not bound by Jewish ceremonial law; foundational for the Gentile mission.
outcome: Gentile converts exempted from circumcision; dietary guidelines issued (Acts 15 decree).

description: Roman general Titus besieged and destroyed Jerusalem and the Temple following the Jewish revolt.
significance: Ended the Jerusalem church's geographic center; scattered the Jewish-Christian community; ended Temple-based Judaism.
outcome: Jerusalem Christians fled to Pella; Jewish-Christian community reconstituted at reduced size.

description: Emperor Nero blamed Christians for the Great Fire of Rome (64 AD) and launched a targeted persecution in the city.
significance: First imperial persecution; traditionally the occasion of Peter's and Paul's martyrdom in Rome.
outcome: Deaths of Peter and Paul; Christian community in Rome survives underground.

description: Emperor Decius ordered all citizens to sacrifice to Roman gods and obtain certificates (libelli). Refusal led to imprisonment and execution.
significance: First empire-wide systematic persecution; produced the lapsed (lapsi) controversy and shaped later penitential theology.
outcome: Widespread apostasy and martyrdoms; Cyprian of Carthage in exile; controversy over readmitting lapsed.

description: The most severe and last major persecution; ordered destruction of churches, burning of scriptures, and imprisonment of clergy.
significance: Produced the largest number of martyrs; led to the Donatist schism in North Africa over traditores; preceded Constantine's legalization.
outcome: Christianity survived; Edict of Toleration (311) by Galerius ended active persecution.

description: Constantine I and Licinius issued a letter granting religious toleration throughout the empire.
significance: Ended official persecution of Christians; enabled public church construction and the Constantinian transformation of Christianity.
outcome: Christianity and all religions granted legal status; confiscated church property returned.

description: Convened by Constantine I with ~300 bishops to resolve the Arian controversy regarding the nature of Christ.
significance: First ecumenical council; produced the Nicene Creed; established canonical principles; set Easter calculation rules.
outcome: Arian position condemned; homoousios affirmed; Arius exiled; Nicene Creed promulgated.

description: Convened by Emperor Theodosius I with 150 bishops to reaffirm Nicaea and address the Pneumatomachi (Spirit-fighters).
significance: Expanded the Nicene Creed to include the Holy Spirit's divinity; declared Constantinople second in honor after Rome.
outcome: Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed promulgated; Apollinarianism and Macedonianism condemned.

description: Bishop Polycarp of Smyrna was arrested, tried, and burned alive at the stadium; the earliest datable Christian martyrdom account.
significance: The Martyrdom of Polycarp established the template for later hagiography and the martyr cult; first dated feast of a saint.
outcome: Polycarp's relics preserved; annual commemoration established; account circulated widely.

description: Ignatius was arrested in Antioch and transported to Rome where he was martyred in the amphitheatre, writing seven letters en route.
significance: The letters became foundational texts for episcopacy, Eucharistic theology, and ecclesiology.
outcome: Seven authentic letters preserved; Ignatius became a model bishop-martyr.

description: Constantine reportedly saw a vision before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge and converted to Christianity.
significance: Transformed Christianity from persecuted sect to imperially favored religion within a generation.
outcome: Constantine defeated Maxentius; began patronage of church building and council convocation.

description: Third ecumenical council convened to address the Nestorian controversy over whether Mary could be called Theotokos.
significance: Defined Mary as Theotokos (God-bearer); condemned Nestorianism; reinforced Christological orthodoxy.
outcome: Nestorius condemned and deposed; Theotokos affirmed; Church of the East separated.

description: Fourth ecumenical council addressing the nature of Christ after the Monophysite controversy.
significance: Defined the two-natures Christology (fully divine and fully human in one person); shaped all subsequent Christological debate.
outcome: Chalcedonian Definition promulgated; Miaphysite churches (Coptic, Armenian, Ethiopian) eventually separated.

description: Germanic king Odoacer deposed the last Western Roman Emperor Romulus Augustulus.
significance: Ended the Western Roman political framework; the church became the primary institutional continuity in the West.
outcome: Papacy assumed increasing civic authority in Rome and Italy; monasticism expanded.

description: The Holy Spirit descended on the apostles at Pentecost; Peter preached and ~3000 were baptized.
significance: Birth of the Christian church; beginning of apostolic preaching and community formation.
outcome: The Jerusalem church was established; communal life described in Acts 2:42-47.

description: Stephen, the first deacon, was stoned to death for blasphemy; Saul (Paul) witnessed and approved.
significance: First Christian martyr; triggered the scattering of the Jerusalem church which spread Christianity beyond Jerusalem.
outcome: Persecution scattered believers to Samaria, Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch (Acts 8, 11).

description: Saul of Tarsus experienced a vision of the risen Christ on the road to Damascus and converted.
significance: Transformed Christianity's chief persecutor into its greatest missionary; pivotal for Gentile expansion.
outcome: Paul began preaching in Damascus, went to Arabia, then returned to Damascus (Gal 1:17).

description: Cyprian convened a council to address the status of Christians who lapsed during the Decian persecution.
significance: Established a graded penitential system for the lapsed; affirmed bishop's authority over penance.
outcome: Moderate position adopted; lapsed could be readmitted after penance, but not without episcopal authorization.

description: African bishops condemned Pelagianism and affirmed Augustine's doctrine of original sin and grace.
significance: Key victory for Augustinian theology; shaped Western doctrine of grace, predestination, and baptism for centuries.
outcome: Pelagius and Caelestius condemned; canons on original sin affirmed.

description: Fifth ecumenical council convened by Justinian I to condemn the Three Chapters and reconcile Chalcedonians with Miaphysites.
significance: Condemned Theodore of Mopsuestia, Theodoret, and Ibas; attempted to heal the Chalcedonian-Miaphysite rift.
outcome: Three Chapters condemned; Chalcedonian Christology reaffirmed with pro-Cyrilline emphasis.

description: Sixth ecumenical council condemned Monothelitism and affirmed that Christ has two wills (dyothelitism).
significance: Vindicated Maximus the Confessor's theology; condemned Pope Honorius I for supporting Monothelitism.
outcome: Monothelitism condemned; two wills and two energies in Christ affirmed.

description: Seventh ecumenical council restored the veneration of icons after the iconoclast controversy.
significance: Ended the first period of iconoclasm; distinguished between veneration (proskynesis) and worship (latreia).
outcome: Iconoclasm condemned; icon veneration restored; John of Damascus vindicated.

description: Perpetua, Felicity, and companions martyred in the arena at Carthage under Septimius Severus.
significance: Perpetua's prison diary is one of the earliest Christian texts by a woman; foundational for martyr cult traditions.
outcome: Account preserved in the Passion of Perpetua and Felicity; widely read in North African churches.

description: Emperor Valerian targeted Christian clergy, senators, and property; Cyprian and Sixtus II of Rome martyred.
significance: Second major empire-wide persecution after Decius; produced prominent episcopal martyrs.
outcome: Cyprian beheaded 258; Pope Sixtus II killed in catacombs; Gallienus ended persecution 260.

description: Pope Gregory the Great sent Augustine of Canterbury with 40 monks to evangelize the Anglo-Saxons.
significance: Established Roman Christianity in England; Canterbury became the seat of English Christianity.
outcome: King Ethelbert of Kent baptized; Canterbury established as episcopal see.

description: Split in the North African church over whether bishops who surrendered scriptures during persecution could validly ordain.
significance: Major ecclesiological controversy that shaped Augustine's theology of sacraments and church unity.
outcome: Donatists condemned at Council of Arles (314) and Conference of Carthage (411); gradually suppressed.
